primary_diagnosis.name: Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis), acute, with high risk of irreversible visual loss

comorbidities.name: Polymyalgia rheumatica (probable overlap — proximal shoulder-girdle stiffness)

pathophysiology_summary: Giant cell arteritis (GCA, temporal arteritis) is a granulomatous large- and medium-vessel vasculitis that characteristically affects the extracranial branches of the external carotid artery, particularly the superficial temporal artery, in adults typically over 50 and most often over 70. Transmural inflammation with giant cells causes intimal hyperplasia and progressive luminal occlusion. Ischaemia of the tissues supplied by involved arteries produces the classic features: new-onset localised temporal headache, scalp tenderness (from involvement of scalp arteries), and jaw claudication (ischaemic fatigue of the masseter and temporalis muscles supplied by branches of the external carotid, coming on with chewing and relieved by rest). Systemic granulomatous inflammation produces constitutional features — fatigue, low-grade fever, weight loss — and a markedly elevated ESR/CRP. A frequent overlap with polymyalgia rheumatica accounts for the proximal shoulder-girdle stiffness. The sight-threatening complication is arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (and, less commonly, central retinal artery occlusion) from involvement of the posterior ciliary/ophthalmic arteries; visual loss is typically sudden, painless, and permanent, and may occur without warning. Crucially, the presence of jaw claudication is one of the features most strongly associated with subsequent visual loss. Because sight loss is irreversible once established, GCA is a medical emergency in which urgent same-day assessment and immediate initiation of high-dose corticosteroid therapy to suppress the inflammation and protect vision take precedence over waiting for confirmatory blood tests or temporal artery biopsy — biopsy remains positive for one to two weeks after steroids are started, so treatment should not be delayed for it.

differential.evidence_basis: A new-onset, severe, localised temporal headache in an older adult with scalp tenderness and jaw pain on chewing must raise immediate concern for GCA even before systemic features and the vision screen are completed, because of the risk of sudden irreversible blindness.

A common cause of headache, but does not explain scalp tenderness of this severity, jaw claudication, or systemic features, and a new severe headache in an older adult should not be attributed to tension headache without excluding arteritis.

TMJ dysfunction can cause jaw pain with chewing, but it is mechanical (clicking/locking) rather than ischaemic fatigue, and does not produce scalp tenderness, a new temporal headache, or systemic inflammatory features.
Can cause severe facial/head pain, but is characteristically brief, sharp, electric-shock-like and triggered by touch, rather than a continuous throbbing ache with systemic features.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor must not settle on tension headache, a dental/TMJ cause, or migraine. A new severe temporal headache with scalp tenderness and jaw pain on chewing in an older adult must place GCA on the differential as important_not_to_miss and trigger active screening for jaw claudication, systemic features, and — critically — any visual symptoms.

differential.evidence_basis: New localised temporal headache (DI-001) + true jaw claudication with rest relief (DI-002) + marked scalp tenderness (DI-003) + constitutional features of fatigue, low-grade fever and weight loss (DI-004) + shoulder-girdle stiffness suggesting PMR overlap (DI-006) in a 72-year-old is a classic GCA constellation. The current absence of visual symptoms (DI-005) does not reduce urgency — it defines the narrow window in which sight can still be protected.

The jaw symptom is ischaemic fatigue relieved by rest, not mechanical joint pain, and is accompanied by scalp tenderness, temporal headache, and systemic inflammatory features that TMJ dysfunction does not explain.
Scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and systemic features are not features of tension headache; the constellation effectively excludes it as the primary diagnosis.
The continuous throbbing character with systemic inflammatory features is not consistent with the brief, paroxysmal, touch-triggered pain of trigeminal neuralgia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After eliciting jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, constitutional features, and screening vision, the AI Doctor should identify a probable arteritic (giant cell arteritis) process as leading and recognise it as a sight-threatening emergency requiring urgent same-day assessment and immediate high-dose corticosteroids — regardless of the fact that biopsy confirmation has not yet occurred and vision is currently intact.

differential.diagnosis: Presumed giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis) with imminent threat to vision
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for the telehealth context. Cannot be confirmed without clinician temporal artery examination, urgent ESR/CRP, and temporal artery biopsy (or temporal artery ultrasound), but the clinical picture is sufficient to mandate urgent same-day assessment and immediate high-dose corticosteroid initiation to protect sight — treatment must not be delayed for confirmatory investigations.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final differential in the telehealth context should be framed as probable GCA constituting a sight-threatening emergency requiring urgent same-day assessment and immediate high-dose steroids — not downgraded to a benign headache cause or deferred to routine review on the basis that vision is currently normal.

red_flags.description: New-onset severe localised temporal headache in an adult over 50, unrelieved by simple analgesia
red_flags.clinical_significance: A new headache of this character in an older adult is a cardinal feature of giant cell arteritis and, in the correct context, mandates urgent evaluation for arteritis rather than attribution to a benign primary headache disorder.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Headache attributed to tension headache or migraine; arteritis not considered; the opportunity to start sight-protecting treatment before visual loss occurs is lost.

red_flags.description: Jaw claudication — ischaemic pain and fatigue of the chewing muscles on chewing, relieved by rest
red_flags.clinical_significance: Jaw claudication is one of the features most strongly associated with GCA and, importantly, with subsequent visual loss. Its presence substantially raises the probability of arteritis and the urgency of treatment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Jaw symptom misattributed to a dental or TMJ problem; the vascular/ischaemic nature and its strong link to impending visual loss are missed; urgent steroid initiation delayed.

red_flags.description: Marked scalp tenderness over the affected temple (on hair-brushing and pillow contact)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Scalp tenderness reflects involvement of the inflamed superficial scalp arteries and is a supportive feature of GCA in this constellation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A discriminating supportive feature is missed, weakening recognition of the arteritic constellation.

red_flags.description: Constitutional inflammatory features — profound fatigue, low-grade fever, and unintentional weight loss (~2kg over 2 weeks)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features reflect the underlying granulomatous inflammatory process and support a systemic inflammatory/arteritic diagnosis over a localised benign headache disorder; they typically accompany a markedly raised ESR/CRP.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The systemic inflammatory nature of the illness is not appreciated; the case may be under-weighted as a purely local headache problem.

red_flags.description: Sudden visual disturbance or loss (blurring, transient loss, a curtain/shadow, or loss of vision in either eye) — currently ABSENT but the imminent, sight-threatening complication to be screened for and safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy causes sudden, usually painless and permanent visual loss and may occur without warning. Vision being intact at presentation is the reason the encounter is a window of opportunity, not a reason to defer — any new visual symptom is an immediate call-000 emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If the vision-loss risk is not communicated and safety-netted, the patient may not recognise a sudden visual change as an emergency and may lose sight permanently that urgent steroids could have protected.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Vision is normal at presentation. The AI Doctor may treat this as a reason to downgrade urgency or defer to routine review, failing to recognise that visual loss in GCA is typically sudden and irreversible and that intact vision now defines the narrow window for sight-protecting treatment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Misattributing jaw claudication to a dental or TMJ cause
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The AI Doctor may interpret jaw pain on chewing as a mechanical joint or dental problem rather than recognising ischaemic claudication (fatigue relieved by rest) as a strong pointer to arteritis and to impending visual loss.

A new headache may be anchored as tension headache or migraine, especially if analgesia is the patient's stated request, missing the age, localisation, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and systemic features that distinguish arteritis.

The AI Doctor may frame the plan around obtaining ESR/CRP and temporal artery biopsy before treatment; in GCA the correct pathway is urgent same-day assessment with immediate high-dose corticosteroids to protect sight, because biopsy remains informative for one to two weeks after steroids are commenced.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attributing systemic symptoms to age or poor sleep
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Fatigue, low-grade fever, weight loss, and shoulder stiffness may be dismissed as ageing or the effect of a bad headache and poor sleep, missing the systemic inflammatory (and PMR-overlap) picture.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of the temporal arteries for a hardened, nodular, tender, pulseless vessel — the key clinical examination sign distinguishing arteritis, obtainable only by a clinician in person
Formal visual acuity, visual field, and relative afferent pupillary defect assessment — cannot be reliably performed over telehealth chat
ESR and CRP — inflammatory markers (typically markedly elevated in GCA) require urgent blood testing that cannot be done via chat
Temporal artery biopsy (and/or temporal artery/large-vessel ultrasound) — required for definitive confirmation, performed in the urgent in-person pathway
Clinician-measured vital signs and formal examination for polymyalgia rheumatica overlap
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day assessment (emergency department or an equivalent urgent same-day medical/ophthalmology pathway) for immediate initiation of high-dose corticosteroid therapy to protect vision, urgent ESR/CRP, and arrangement of temporal artery biopsy/ultrasound — do not delay treatment for confirmatory tests. Call 000 immediately if any visual change develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating this as a benign headache or a dental/TMJ problem, or deferring to routine review because vision is currently intact, risks sudden and permanent blindness that immediate high-dose corticosteroids could have prevented.
